EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact, well-defined, upper level disturbance tracked along and south of Interstate 94 during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th. It was an extremely slow moving feature, as wind speeds were very weak. Thunderstorms that were associated with it produced heavy rain, funnel clouds, and isolated strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boat was sunk by the heavy rain. The rain guage read over four inches, but was blocked by leaves, so an accurate reading was impossible.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several secondary and county roads were overtopped and damaged due to flood waters. The Leaf River was reported flowing upstream due to higher downstream water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms passed through Grant County during the early morning hours of the 24th. This rainfall may have helped to mix some of the stronger winds aloft down to the surface. Several pine trees in the Herman area were blown over by these strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several ten to twenty foot tall pine trees were blown over during a protracted period of heavy rain and strong gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early morning hours of the 4th, an area of surface low pressure had moved into northeast South Dakota, with a warm front extending out to the east. Surface dew point values north of the front ranged in the lower 60s while south of the front they topped out around 70. At 850 millibars, the low level jet pushed juicy air up into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, with drier air to the north. Finally, forty-five to fifty knot westerly winds at 500 millibars provided ample shear to the system. Thunderstorms over western and central North Dakota proceeded east and moved into Barnes County, North Dakota, during the first few hours of the 4th. These storms tracked nearly due east, finally exiting Wadena County, Minnesota, around sunrise. The strongest storms mainly stayed along and south of Interstate 94 in North Dakota and U. S. Highway 10 in Minnesota. These storms trained across the same areas as well, with some spots experiencing large hail or damaging winds two or three times during the event. In addition, heavy rainfall became a problem, resulting in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were flooded in south Moorhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms developed during the late afternoon and early evening of September 20th ahead of cold front that was moving across southeast Minnesota. Strong winds from these storms damaged trees in Austin (Mower County), Ostrander (Fillmore County) and Cherry Grove (Fillmore County). These storms also dropped quarter sized hail in Ostrander and Cherry Grove.

During the late afternoon of July 21st, a stationary boundary set up across northeast North Dakota. Temperatures north of the boundary were generally in the low to mid 80s, while to the south they were in the mid 80s to low 90s. Surface dew point readings were very high, with most locations in the low to mid 70s. A few thunderstorms fired north of the front around the Langdon, ND, area, dropping some large hail. However, the more significant action came from a thunderstorm complex that formed across west central North Dakota and moved east during the evening. This complex evolved into a bow echo with good rotational structure on the northern book end vortice. Strong straight line winds, large hail, and a few tornadoes were reported as this complex moved across eastern North Dakota into the northwest quarter of Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind was measured at the Thief River Falls Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon of September 19th, scattered thunderstorms developed across southern Manitoba. These storms seemed to develop around a weak surface low that had lingered over the northern Red River Valley through the afternoon. One thunderstorm crossed into Pembina County in northeast North Dakota, and immediately showed tornadic tendencies. As this storm crossed into Kittson County in northwest Minnesota it produced a tornado. Other storms resulted in a second tornado as well as several reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind was measured by the NDAWN mesonet station along county road 4. The winds were likely induced by the passing tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet dipped into the northern plains during the afternoon of the 20th, accompanied by a strong upper level disturbance. These features produced showers and thunderstorms over southeast Saskatchewan (Canada) that morning that moved into north central and northeast North Dakota during the afternoon. As these showers and thunderstorms moved into northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota, they developed into a continuous line with some bowing segments. The forward speed on the line was about 45 to 50 mph and mesonet observations picked up wind gusts from 50 to 60 mph. Other showers and thunderstorms developed south of this line, or generally along and south of Interstate 94. Some of these storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few four to six inch diameter tree branches and tree trunks were snapped off along Two Inlets Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced nickel size hail and heavy rain near Tyler in Lincoln County in southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon of August 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.50 inches was accompanied by nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak funnel cloud was observed by several people near Windom in Cottonwood County in southwest Minnesota during the afternoon of August 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed by several people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Tuesday, March 4th, a band of snow developed along the Minnesota River Valley. The snowfall became heavy at times during the late evening, and produced locally 6 to 11 inches from near Milroy in western Redwood County, southeast to Albert Lea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick snow storm produced locally 9 inches of snow near Milroy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the eastern Dakotas during the early morning of Monday, May 19th. The southern edge of these storms moved across far west central Minnesota and produced large hail from near Lake Oliver to Danvers in western Swift County. These storms quickly weakened as they moved into central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was the size of pennies and nickels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms developed during the late afternoon and early evening of September 20th ahead of cold front that was moving across southeast Minnesota. Strong winds from these storms damaged trees in Austin (Mower County), Ostrander (Fillmore County) and Cherry Grove (Fillmore County). These storms also dropped quarter sized hail in Ostrander and Cherry Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Ostrander, 6 inch diameter trees branches were snapped off by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet dipped into the northern plains during the afternoon of the 20th, accompanied by a strong upper level disturbance. These features produced showers and thunderstorms over southeast Saskatchewan (Canada) that morning that moved into north central and northeast North Dakota during the afternoon. As these showers and thunderstorms moved into northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota, they developed into a continuous line with some bowing segments. The forward speed on the line was about 45 to 50 mph and mesonet observations picked up wind gusts from 50 to 60 mph. Other showers and thunderstorms developed south of this line, or generally along and south of Interstate 94. Some of these storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Abbey Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of July 21st, a stationary boundary set up across northeast North Dakota. Temperatures north of the boundary were generally in the low to mid 80s, while to the south they were in the mid 80s to low 90s. Surface dew point readings were very high, with most locations in the low to mid 70s. A few thunderstorms fired north of the front around the Langdon, ND, area, dropping some large hail. However, the more significant action came from a thunderstorm complex that formed across west central North Dakota and moved east during the evening. This complex evolved into a bow echo with good rotational structure on the northern book end vortice. Strong straight line winds, large hail, and a few tornadoes were reported as this complex moved across eastern North Dakota into the northwest quarter of Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A river gaging station on the highway 1 bridge was damaged by the wind. The antenna had missing and bent tines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first two days of March remained extremely cold, with wind chill values dropping to -35F or colder for several hours during the the evening of March 1st, to the morning hours of March 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of surface observations indicated that temperatures and wind speeds were cold enough to produced wind chill values of -35F or colder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of sub-zero temperatures and north winds of 15 to 25 mph produced bitter cold wind chills of 35 below to 40 below zero. Several area activities were cancelled, as well as some schools on the 27th. Some of the coldest wind chills include; 41 below near Graceville and 37 below in Ortonville.

A clipper system passing across the region brought light snowfall and very strong northwest winds gusting in excess of 50 mph resulting in blizzard conditions.  The highest wind gusts include; 55 mph near Graceville and 46 mph in Ortonville.  Blizzard conditions ended during the late afternoon hours.  The blizzard led to the cancellation of several area activities and schools along with dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota was hit by a blizzard during the afternoon of January 26th. Sustained winds of 25 to 40 mph with gusts approaching 60 mph produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting to occur making many roads impassable and several vehicles were trapped in the snow. The stage was set for the blizzard to occur when a system passed across the region during the evening of the 25th and early morning of 26th and produced 2 to 3 inches of light fluffy snow. This snow then was used by the winds, along with additional falling light snow, to create the blizzard conditions behind a strong area of low pressure that moved across central Minnesota and northern Wisconsin. The highest measured wind gust was 58 mph from the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts, created considerable blowing and drifting snow across Winona County after 2 to 3 inches of snow fell. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions. All the schools across the county were closed on the 27th as a result of the conditions produced by the blowing and drifting snow and extremely cold wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of wind and cold temperatures resulted in a period of widespread dangerously cold wind chills around -40 to -45 degrees across much of northeast Minnesota.

The combination of Arctic air and winds up to 15 mph (higher in some areas) resulted in a prolonged period of dangerously cold wind chill temperatures of 40 to 60 degrees below zero across northeast Minnesota. Southeast Minnesota was hit by a blizzard during the afternoon of January 26th. Sustained winds of 25 to 40 mph with gusts approaching 60 mph produced widespread whiteout conditions where the visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less. The winds also caused severe drifting to occur making many roads impassable and several vehicles were trapped in the snow. The stage was set for the blizzard to occur when a system passed across the region during the evening of the 25th and early morning of 26th and produced 2 to 3 inches of light fluffy snow. This snow then was used by the winds, along with additional falling light snow, to create the blizzard conditions behind a strong area of low pressure that moved across central Minnesota and northern Wisconsin. The highest measured wind gust was 58 mph from the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts, created considerable blowing and drifting snow across Houston County after 2 to 3 inches of snow fell. The blowing and drifting snow created very poor driving conditions. All the schools across the county were closed on the 27th as a result of the conditions produced by the blowing and drifting snow and extremely cold wind chills.

The highest measured wind gust during the blizzard was 54 mph from a mesonet reporting station near Dexter.

A cold front swept through the area during the early morning of the 16th, as surface low pressure tracked across southern Canada into the Minnesota arrowhead. Behind the cold front, temperatures dropped quickly and northwest winds increased. Many locations across the area saw wind gusts in the 45 to 60 mph range. U. S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston due to whiteout conditions. Quite a few schools and events were also closed during the storm. Conditions were the worst in open country when snow was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across southeast Minnesota from the evening of January 5th into the late evening of the 6th or early morning of the 7th. The coldest wind chills of 40 to 55 below occurred on the morning of the 6th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 4th and 5th which dropped the air temperatures well below zero with strong northwest winds. The lowest recorded wind chill was 55 below from a mesonet reporting station near Dexter (Mower County). The Governor of Minnesota order all schools across the state to be closed on January 6th. All the schools across southeast Minnesota were once again closed on the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Dodge County from the evening of January 5th through the evening of the 6th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 52 below from a mesonet reporting station near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system which began across the Northern Rockies Monday, dived southeast over the Plains, and organized across the Upper Midwest Tuesday and Tuesday night, March 18th. Initially, light snow began across west central and central Minnesota after midnight, Tuesday. Locally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell between midnight and sunrise. During the morning, a small but intense band of snow developed across west central and east central Minnesota between Montevideo, and the northern suburbs of the Twin Cities. This band of snow, drifted northward during the morning...and produced heavy snowfall from near Madison, northeast to St. Cloud and Mora, Minnesota. By Tuesday afternoon, locally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell near Madison, east-northeast to Mora, Minnesota. The snowfall slowly tapered off during the late afternoon and moved  east-southeast across eastern Minnesota where locally 2 to 5 inches fell the evening of Tuesday, March 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning of Tuesday, March 18th. Heavy snow fell during the mid to late morning hours, before tapering off during the afternoon. Locally six inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 45 below were common across southeast Minnesota on the mornings of January 27th and 28th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 26th. As temperatures fell behind the front, strong northwest winds starting pushing the wind chills to 35 below or colder during the early morning of the 27th. Most locations then had wind chills of 35 below or colder into the morning of the 28th, which was when warmer air started to spread back across the area. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 below from a mesonet reporting station near Dexter (Mower County). All the schools across southeast Minnesota were closed January 27th. A majority of the schools were closed again on the 28th or delayed the start of classes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Dodge County from the early morning of January 27th through the morning of the 28th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 44 below from a mesonet reporting station near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest air in recent history moved into the region during the afternoon hours of the 5th and continued into the afternoon hours of the 6th. The combination of sub-zero temperatures with north winds produced dangerously cold wind chills from 45 below to 50 degrees below zero. Winds gusted to over 40 mph at times. Several area activities were cancelled, as well as several schools on Monday the 6th. Some of the lowest wind chills include; 45 degrees below at Wheaton and 48 degrees below zero at Ortonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 45 below were common across southeast Minnesota on the mornings of January 27th and 28th. These dangerous wind chills occurred behind a strong cold front that moved across the region on January 26th. As temperatures fell behind the front, strong northwest winds starting pushing the wind chills to 35 below or colder during the early morning of the 27th. Most locations then had wind chills of 35 below or colder into the morning of the 28th, which was when warmer air started to spread back across the area. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 below from a mesonet reporting station near Dexter (Mower County). All the schools across southeast Minnesota were closed January 27th. A majority of the schools were closed again on the 28th or delayed the start of classes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 below or colder occurred across Winona County from the early morning of January 27th into the morning of the 28th. The lowest recorded wind chill was 42 below from a mesonet reporting station near Ridgeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system which began across the Northern Rockies Monday, dived southeast over the Plains, and organized across the Upper Midwest Tuesday and Tuesday night, March 18th. Initially, light snow began across west central and central Minnesota after midnight, Tuesday. Locally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell between midnight and sunrise. During the morning, a small but intense band of snow developed across west central and east central Minnesota between Montevideo, and the northern suburbs of the Twin Cities. This band of snow, drifted northward during the morning...and produced heavy snowfall from near Madison, northeast to St. Cloud and Mora, Minnesota. By Tuesday afternoon, locally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell near Madison, east-northeast to Mora, Minnesota. The snowfall slowly tapered off during the late afternoon and moved  east-southeast across eastern Minnesota where locally 2 to 5 inches fell the evening of Tuesday, March 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning of Tuesday, March 18th. Heavy snow fell during the mid to late morning hours, before tapering off during the afternoon. Locally six to eight inches fell across the northern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the area during the early morning of the 16th, as surface low pressure tracked across southern Canada into the Minnesota arrowhead. Behind the cold front, temperatures dropped quickly and northwest winds increased. Many locations across the area saw wind gusts in the 45 to 60 mph range. U. S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston due to whiteout conditions. Quite a few schools and events were also closed during the storm. Conditions were the worst in open country when snow was falling.

A small area of intense snowfall resulted in over 6 inches of snow across south central Minnesota from Fairmont, to Mankato to around Faribault Wednesday morning between 3 am and noon.  ||Initially, an area of low pressure developed across the Northern Plains Tuesday evening. This area of low pressure moved southeast across South Dakota, and into central Iowa by Wednesday morning, and into eastern Missouri by Wednesday evening. Light snow began to develop across southwest and south central Minnesota after midnight, with moderate to locally heavy snowfall by sunrise. Several bands of heavy snow, with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour, occurred during the morning before tapering off to light snow by noon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 6 inches or more of snow fell across Blue Earth County. The heaviest occurred near Amboy which measured 9.3 inches by the afternoon.

An early winter storm brought a record snowfall amount, on November 10th, to St. Cloud, Minnesota with 13.2 inches.  This is a daily record as well as the most snowfall in any 24 hour period for any November. ||St. Cloud, Minnesota ended with a total of 13.5 inches of snowfall for this event.  ||This storm began to develop across the Dakotas late Sunday evening, and quickly moved east and northeast across southern Minnesota and into central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin by early Monday morning, November 10th.  The surface weather map had a developing area of low pressure across Nebraska Sunday evening. This low pressure center moved east-northeast across Iowa, and into the Great Lakes region by Monday night. ||Light snow began across west central Minnesota prior to midnight. Another area of light snow developed across south central Minnesota and quickly moved northward across east central and central Minnesota by sunrise. There was a sharp cut off between heavy snowfall, and only an inch or two of snow as this system intensified. There was even a period of sleet and freezing rain on the southern edge of the snowfall which created very icy conditions on area roadways. The mixed precipitation was mainly south of a line from Granite Falls to Litchfield, Buffalo, Anoka and Stillwater, Minnesota. North of this line, heavy snow fell, with the heaviest band from Madison to Willmar, St. Cloud and Rush City. These areas received between a foot and a foot and a half with isolated higher amounts.  ||Although the worst conditions were between 6 am and noon across west central, central and east central Minnesota, snowfall continued into the afternoon for areas along and north of I-94. The snow began to taper off during the late afternoon, but additional snow started later in the evening with a secondary system moving across the Upper Midwest. However, the bulk of the winter storm fell between 2 am to 3 pm Monday. ||Here are a few higher snowfall totals from this snow storm.||16.5 inches at Cambridge.|16.5 inches at St. Augusta.|15.5 inches near North Branch. |15.0 inches northeast of Rush City. |15.0 inches at Starbuck.|14.5 inches east of Princeton.  |13.8 inches at Milaca. |13.6 inches north of Kimball. |13.5 inches at St. Cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers, local officials and the general public measured or estimated between 6 to 8 inches on the south side of Anoka County, to over a foot in the northern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moved from central Nebraska and through Wisconsin. The low brought widespread snowfall of 6 to 9 to parts of east central and northeast Minnesota, but as much as 14 of snow near Pine City. Other snowfall reports included 9 near Hinckley, 11.5 near Moose Lake, 9.2 near Malmo, nearly 11 near Holyoke, more than 8 near Duluth, and 7 near Fort Ripley.

An arctic air mass settled over the Northern Plains bringing well below normal temperatures with it. Daytime highs generally ranged in the teens below zero with lows in the 20s below to 30s below zero. With steady winds, wind chill temperatures dipped into the 40s below to 60s below zero. The Minnesota Governor ordered all schools closed on Monday the 6th as a precaution.

A low pressure moved from central Nebraska and through Wisconsin. The low brought widespread snowfall of 6 to 9 to parts of east central and northeast Minnesota, but as much as 14 of snow near Pine City. Other snowfall reports included 9 near Hinckley, 11.5 near Moose Lake, 9.2 near Malmo, nearly 11 near Holyoke, more than 8 near Duluth, and 7 near Fort Ripley.

A small area of intense snowfall resulted in over 6 inches of snow across south central Minnesota from Fairmont, to Mankato to around Faribault Wednesday morning between 3 am and noon.  ||Initially, an area of low pressure developed across the Northern Plains Tuesday evening. This area of low pressure moved southeast across South Dakota, and into central Iowa by Wednesday morning, and into eastern Missouri by Wednesday evening. Light snow began to develop across southwest and south central Minnesota after midnight, with moderate to locally heavy snowfall by sunrise. Several bands of heavy snow, with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour, occurred during the morning before tapering off to light snow by noon. One intense band of snow occurred from near Mankato to Faribault, where locally 11 inches of snow fell Wednesday morning west of Faribault.  ||The following are some higher snowfall amounts from this storm:||11.0 inches just west of Faribault.|9.3 inches near Amboy.|8.6 inches near Blue Earth.|8.5 inches near Fairmont. |8.4 inches near Eagle Lake.|8.0 inches near Winnebago. |8.0 inches near Vernon Center. |7.5 inches near Le Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 6 inches or snow fell across Steele County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 6 inches or snow fell across Waseca County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass settled over the Northern Plains bringing well below normal temperatures with it. Daytime highs generally ranged in the teens below zero with lows in the 20s below to 30s below zero. With steady winds, wind chill temperatures dipped into the 40s below to 60s below zero. The Minnesota Governor ordered all schools closed on Monday the 6th as a precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm brought a record snowfall amount, on November 10th, to St. Cloud, Minnesota with 13.2 inches.  This is a daily record as well as the most snowfall in any 24 hour period for any November. ||St. Cloud, Minnesota ended with a total of 13.5 inches of snowfall for this event.  ||This storm began to develop across the Dakotas late Sunday evening, and quickly moved east and northeast across southern Minnesota and into central Minnesota and west central Wisconsin by early Monday morning, November 10th.  The surface weather map had a developing area of low pressure across Nebraska Sunday evening. This low pressure center moved east-northeast across Iowa, and into the Great Lakes region by Monday night. ||Light snow began across west central Minnesota prior to midnight. Another area of light snow developed across south central Minnesota and quickly moved northward across east central and central Minnesota by sunrise. There was a sharp cut off between heavy snowfall, and only an inch or two of snow as this system intensified. There was even a period of sleet and freezing rain on the southern edge of the snowfall which created very icy conditions on area roadways. The mixed precipitation was mainly south of a line from Granite Falls to Litchfield, Buffalo, Anoka and Stillwater, Minnesota. North of this line, heavy snow fell, with the heaviest band from Madison to Willmar, St. Cloud and Rush City. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers, local officials and the general public measured or estimated between 8 to 12 inches on the far northeast side of the county, to around an inch in the south. in addition, some sleet and freezing rain mixed with the snow during the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass settled over the Northern Plains bringing well below normal temperatures with it. Daytime highs generally ranged in the teens below zero with lows in the 20s below to 30s below zero. With steady winds, wind chill temperatures dipped into the 40s below to 60s below zero. The Minnesota Governor ordered all schools closed on Monday the 6th as a precaution.

An abnormally warm second week of December kept temperatures from dropping below freezing across a large part of the Upper Midwest. This allowed for most of the snow cover to melt, which kept the boundary layer warm and moist when a storm system began to organize across the Plains on the 14th. A cold front moved into far western Minnesota during the morning of Monday, December 15th.  Temperatures started to drop from the 30s into the 20s by the late morning and afternoon hours. As moisture began to surge northward ahead of the storm system, precipitation fell mostly as rain, with some sleet. ||As temperatures fell below freezing, area roadways became ice covered. Area web cams and the Minnesota Department of Transportation showed ice covered roads in Yellow Medicine, Lac Qui Parle and Chippewa Counties by the afternoon. ||The freezing rain, sleet and snow changed over to sleet and snow by the early afternoon in far western Minnesota, and slowly spread eastward. By sunset, most of west central, central and southwest Minnesota had snow as the main form of precipitation. By morning, an area of 1 to 2 inches of snow fell on the ice covered roadways. Some areas received around one quarter inch of ice before changing over to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began to fall across western Lac Qui Parle County by the mid to late morning. By the afternoon, all roadways were ice covered, with some areas receiving around one quarter of an inch of freezing rain.  Snow started falling during the afternoon, along with gusty northwest winds.  This caused some white out conditions during the evening before the winds lessened overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle preceded snow in southwest Minnesota on December 15th. The freezing precipitation began during the morning hours and produced significant ice accumulations. Precipitation changed to snow during the day and light snow continued into the evening. Ice storm and winter storm conditions were produced in some areas, either for ice accumulations alone, or for the combination of ice and snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations, estimated at one quarter inch. The freezing rain was followed by 1 to 2 inches of snow, including 2 inches at Tyler. Strong northwest winds produced some drifting snow. Roads were made very icy, there was damage to power lines and resulting power outages, and school classes were cancelled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches in parts of southwest Minnesota during the evening of December 26th and after midnight on December 27th. The snow was accompanied by winds of 15 to 25 mph which caused minor drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches, including 3.3 inches at Luverne. The snow was accompanied by minor drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the area during the early morning of the 16th, as surface low pressure tracked across southern Canada into the Minnesota arrowhead. Behind the cold front, temperatures dropped quickly and northwest winds increased. Many locations across the area saw wind gusts in the 45 to 60 mph range. U. S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston due to whiteout conditions. Quite a few schools and events were also closed during the storm. Conditions were the worst in open country when snow was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures across southeast Minnesota contributed to the death of an elderly lady in Rochester (Olmsted County). The woman wandered away from her house on the evening of December 2nd without her coat or purse. She spent the night outside and was found deceased the next morning. Temperatures were in the teens and 20s while the woman was outdoors. The woman was believed to have medical conditions that contributed to her death along with the exposure to the cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly lady passed away after spending the night of December 2nd outdoors with temperatures in the teens and 20s. The lady had medical conditions that likely contributed to her death along with the exposure to the cold temperatures.

A woman was killed in a December 20th traffic accident near Pilot Mound (Fillmore County) that occurred on icy roads. The icy roads developed from moisture that was deposited on the roads from fog with temperatures below freezing. According to police reports, the vehicle the woman was riding in went off the road because of the icy conditions and rolled into a ditch. The automated weather observing equipment at the Preston airport was reporting 3 miles visibility in fog with a temperature of 28 degrees at the time of the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was killed in a one car accident that occurred near Pilot Mound during the evening of December 20th. Icy conditions developed from moisture deposited on the roads from fog with temperatures below freezing. The vehicle the woman was riding in went off the road because of the icy conditions and rolled into a ditch.

In Grant County, six inches fell at Elbow Lake and thirteen inches fell at Hoffman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds gusting to 45 to 55 mph combined with several inches of fresh powdery snow on the ground to produce whiteout conditions in open country. Conditions also deteriorated in some sections of the larger cities, which had not happened with the other blizzard events of the winter to date. Interstate 94 was closed from Bismarck through the Fargo-Moorhead area to Alexandria. Interstate 29 was closed throughout all of eastern North Dakota, U. S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston, and U. S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes. Some schools were delayed or cancelled on the 27th, as roads were just starting to be plowed.

A very cold air mass settled over the region, with morning lows on the 1st generally in the 20s below zero. Highs on the 1st only managed to rise to around 10 below zero. New record low maximum temperatures were set at Fargo and Grand Forks for the 1st. As high pressure drifted into southern Minnesota on the morning of the 2nd, temperatures once again dropped into the teens and 20s below zero. Steady northwest winds combined with these very cold temperatures to produce wind chill readings from 40 below to 55 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds gusting to 45 to 55 mph combined with several inches of fresh powdery snow on the ground to produce whiteout conditions in open country. Conditions also deteriorated in some sections of the larger cities, which had not happened with the other blizzard events of the winter to date. Interstate 94 was closed from Bismarck through the Fargo-Moorhead area to Alexandria. Interstate 29 was closed throughout all of eastern North Dakota, U. S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston, and U. S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes. Some schools were delayed or cancelled on the 27th, as roads were just starting to be plowed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet fell across the area on the afternoon of the 3rd, helping to put a little crust on the old snowpack. As the winds increased during the late afternoon and evening, several light snow bands also moved through. As these snow bands moved through, visibilities dropped to near zero at times in open country. Conditions generally improved when the snow ended, indicating the crust on the old snow mainly held, not allowing the old snow to blow around too. Several major roads were closed, including Interstate 29 between Grand Forks and Fargo and U. S. Highway 2 between East Grand Forks and Crookston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 5 inches in parts of Lincoln and Lyon Counties in southwest Minnesota during the late morning and afternoon of March 18th. North winds gusting to 30 mph caused  a few areas of blowing snow and some minor drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of surface low pressure moving across the region brought a heavy snow band into west central Minnesota.  Snowfall amounts included, 6 inches in Correll.

A potent surface low pressure area tracking across northern Minnesota along with a strong cold front dropping across our region brought very strong northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph.  The very strong winds along with the existing snow cover brought widespread blizzard conditions across west central Minnesota resulting in significant travel problems.

A period of northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph at times combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause blizzard conditions from late morning to the start of the evening on January 16th. The blizzard caused widespread visibilities of less than one quarter mile in open areas. Travel was brought to a temporary halt and schools and some businesses were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause widespread frequent visibilities below a quarter mile in blowing snow. Schools and some businesses closed as travel temporarily became difficult to impossible.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause widespread frequent visibilities below a quarter mile in blowing snow. Schools and some businesses closed as travel temporarily became difficult to impossible.

A very cold air mass settled over the region, with morning lows on the 1st generally in the 20s below zero. Highs on the 1st only managed to rise to around 10 below zero. New record low maximum temperatures were set at Fargo and Grand Forks for the 1st. As high pressure drifted into southern Minnesota on the morning of the 2nd, temperatures once again dropped into the teens and 20s below zero. Steady northwest winds combined with these very cold temperatures to produce wind chill readings from 40 below to 55 below zero.

As surface low pressure moved into the western Great Lakes, north-northwest winds became very gusty in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Gusts from 40 to 55 mph produced whiteout conditions in the immediate northern Red River Valley. Even the normally sheltered areas of southern Beltrami County experienced low visibilities. Spotters from Bemidji reported near whiteout conditions at times. As wind speeds decreased by late evening, visibilities improved.

North to northwest winds gusting to 45 to 55 mph combined with several inches of fresh powdery snow on the ground to produce whiteout conditions in open country. Conditions also deteriorated in some sections of the larger cities, which had not happened with the other blizzard events of the winter to date. Interstate 94 was closed from Bismarck through the Fargo-Moorhead area to Alexandria. Interstate 29 was closed throughout all of eastern North Dakota, U. S. Highway 2 was closed from East Grand Forks to Crookston, and U. S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes. Some schools were delayed or cancelled on the 27th, as roads were just starting to be plowed.

Wet snow accumulated 6 inches or more in part of southwest Minnesota from the afternoon of April 3rd to the pre-dawn hours of April 4th. The heaviest snow of 9.7 inches fell near Windom. Northwest winds increasing to 15 to 30 mph caused some minor drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 3 to 7 inches, with the heavier amounts in the southeast part of Murray County. An accumulation of 6 inches was reported near Fulda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of storms developed during the early morning hours of June 19th along a warm front across southeast Minnesota. Locally heavy rains from these storms caused water to go over a county road south of Austin (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains and inflow from tributary streams pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Winona. The river crested over two feet above the flood stage at 15.52 feet. The river remained above flood stage into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of storms developed during the early morning hours of June 19th along a warm front across southeast Minnesota. Locally heavy rains from these storms caused water to go over a county road south of Austin (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused water to go over County Road 105 south of Austin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 4 inches combined with northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph to cause areas of blowing snow. Visibilities lowered locally and briefly to less than a quarter mile, and more frequently to one quarter to one mile. The snow and blowing snow were during the night of February 20th and 21st, with conditions improving several hours before daybreak on February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches, including 3 inches at Windom, was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph. This combination caused areas of blowing snow, with visibilities lowering locally and briefly to less than a quarter mile. More frequent was the lowering of visibilities to near or a little below one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 6 inches or more in part of southwest Minnesota from the afternoon of April 3rd to the pre-dawn hours of April 4th. The heaviest snow of 9.7 inches fell near Windom. Northwest winds increasing to 15 to 30 mph caused some minor drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 5 to 10 inches, with an accumulation of 9.7 inches reported just south of Windom. Some schools in the county were delayed starting.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 2 to 7 inches, with the heavier accumulations in the southern and eastern parts of Rock County. An accumulation of 6 inches was reported just north northeast of Luverne.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours of June 23rd across southeast Minnesota. Some of these storms produced funnel clouds near Stewartville (Olmsted County) and Pickwick (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three funnel clouds were sighted north of Stewartville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was sighted northwest of Pickwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 26th over southeast Minnesota. At least one funnel cloud was sighted near Wasioja (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Wasioja.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the morning hours of June 27th. These storms dumped enough rain to cause a mud slide to occur north of Lanesboro (Fillmore County) that went across State Highway 250.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet automated weather observing system measured 4.13 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Harmony, 3.74 inches of rain was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms intensified on the southern edge of a line of convection, which extended from the northern end of the Red River Valley southward into west central Minnesota. One storm over Grant County, Minnesota, produced quarter sized hail.
Thunderstorms flared up in the afternoon across west central Minnesota, near a cold front. Large hail fell southeast of Henning, in Otter Tail County.

A narrow line of showers and thunderstorms formed from Fergus Falls to just north of Park Rapids, dropping two to three inches of rain in about an hour. These were slow moving storms, and by the time the rain ended, total rainfall amounts ranged from about two to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water in some streets rose to two to three feet deep and caused storm sewers to overflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, severe storms rumbled across southeast Minnesota. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of an advancing cold front. These storms primarily produced large hail, but a shed and tree were blown down by strong winds near West Concord (Dodge County). The largest hail reported was quarter sized in Stewartville (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Stewartville.

Wet snow accumulated 6 inches or more in part of southwest Minnesota from the afternoon of April 3rd to the pre-dawn hours of April 4th. The heaviest snow of 9.7 inches fell near Windom. Northwest winds increasing to 15 to 30 mph caused some minor drifting of the snow.

An area of surface low pressure intensified as it moved into southeast South Dakota on the morning of the 31st. This resulted in gusty north to northeast winds of 25 to 35 mph and heavy amounts of snow in some areas. Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of May 7th north of a slowly advancing warm front. These storms produced some large hail across portions of Mower, Dodge and Winona Counties. The largest reported hail was golf ball sized near Waltham (Mower County) and in Hayfield (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in Hayfield.

The snow and blowing snow were during the night of February 20th and 21st, with conditions improving several hours before daybreak on February 21st.

More frequent was the lowering of visibilities to near or a little below one mile.

A small thunderstorm complex moved slowly across Martin County during the early evening of Sunday, April 6th. A lightning bolt struck a sign and blew a hole in the ground, at the Fairmont Municipal Airport. Multiple bulbs were blown out, and the underground transformers and wiring were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning bolt struck a sign and blew a hole in the ground, at the Fairmont Municipal Airport. Multiple bulbs were blown out, and the underground transformers and wiring were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary front during the early morning hours of June 18th. As these storms moved across southeast Minnesota they primarily produced rain, but a couple of storms become strong enough to blow down some trees in Brownsdale (Mower County) and drop some hail in Dexter (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Zumbro River out of its banks sending water over County Road 105 isolating Oxbow County Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the morning hours of June 27th. These storms dumped enough rain to cause a mud slide to occur north of Lanesboro (Fillmore County) that went across State Highway 250.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks twice in the Whitewater State Park. The first crest was around three quarters of a foot above the flood stage at 13.78 feet and the second crest was almost a foot above the flood stage at 14.43 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 26th over southeast Minnesota. At least one funnel cloud was sighted near Wasioja (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains and inflow from tributary streams pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Lake City. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 16.43 feet. The river remained above flood stage into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of storms developed during the early morning hours of June 19th along a warm front across southeast Minnesota. Locally heavy rains from these storms caused water to go over a county road south of Austin (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains and inflow from tributary streams pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Wabasha. The river crested over two feet above the flood stage at 14.37 feet. The river remained above flood stage into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary front during the early morning hours of June 18th. As these storms moved across southeast Minnesota they primarily produced rain, but a couple of storms become strong enough to blow down some trees in Brownsdale (Mower County) and drop some hail in Dexter (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were uprooted by thunderstorm winds in Brownsdale. Another weaker tree fell on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary front during the early morning hours of June 18th. As these storms moved across southeast Minnesota they primarily produced rain, but a couple of storms become strong enough to blow down some trees in Brownsdale (Mower County) and drop some hail in Dexter (Mower County).
Thunderstorms produced a tornado and heavy rain in the late evening of June 18th and morning of June 19th. The heavy rain caused flash flooding and areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of fields and other lowlands, including numerous roads, and several homes and businesses. This flooding lasted through most of the daytime hours. Some roads were damaged or washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along and ahead of a cold front that was located over central North Dakota late in the afternoon of the 21st. The thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell along highway 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance stalled over northwestern Minnesota during the early mornings hours of June 24th. This resulted in persistent showers and thunderstorms across portions of extreme northwest Minnesota. Two to five inches of rain fell over a six to ten hour period, causing flooding issues across Kittson and Marshall counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fields and farm access roads were flooded between Karlstad and Halma in western Deerwood Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance stalled over northwestern Minnesota during the early mornings hours of June 24th. This resulted in persistent showers and thunderstorms across portions of extreme northwest Minnesota. Two to five inches of rain fell over a six to ten hour period, causing flooding issues across Kittson and Marshall counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Middle River in the town of Middle River, causing a few streets to be closed and several spots to be sandbagged. Farm fields and farm access roads were also flooded near the Middle River in eastern Spruce Valley and western Cedar townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of June 20th, a slow moving upper level low pressure system was located over southern Saskatchewan. Strong upper level winds combined with a cold pool aloft to help generate scattered thunderstorms across the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Reports of large hail and funnel clouds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent wall cloud with large funnel was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of July 21st, a stationary boundary set up across northeast North Dakota. Temperatures north of the boundary were generally in the low to mid 80s, while to the south they were in the mid 80s to low 90s. Surface dew point readings were very high, with most locations in the low to mid 70s. A few thunderstorms fired north of the front around the Langdon, ND, area, dropping some large hail. However, the more significant action came from a thunderstorm complex that formed across west central North Dakota and moved east during the evening. This complex evolved into a bow echo with good rotational structure on the northern book end vortice. Strong straight line winds, large hail, and a few tornadoes were reported as this complex moved across eastern North Dakota into the northwest quarter of Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a wind gust of 80 mph. The strong winds wrecked havoc on the trees in the city of Crookston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of weak tornadoes and funnel clouds developed along a line of thunderstorms across west central Minnesota during the late afternoon of Thursday, June 19th. All tornadoes and funnel clouds were quite narrow, and some of them occurred simultaneously or nearly so.  There was no significant damage nor any injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down southwest of Benson.  It moved northeast, occasionally lifting up and coming back down to the ground.  It crossed Highway 12 where it knocked down or broke a few trees.  It continued to the northeast, and knocked down or broke about a dozen trees in the golf course on the northwest edge of Benson.  It was photographed and videotaped by numerous citizens, fire department personnel and law enforcement.  There were two other adjacent funnel clouds near the tornado, but they did not touch down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in a field and moved northeast-northeast across County Road 13.  It only tore up some field grasses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and multiple witnesses watched this tornado touch down in an open field. It was described as being like for a rope. After the tornado lifted, the funnel descended another two times but never again made contact with the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed late Tuesday evening along an elevated frontal boundary across far southern Minnesota. Initially, torrential rainfall caused flooding around New Ulm and Mankato, then it evolved into a wind damage threat as it moved eastward across south central Minnesota. Significant damage occurred in Albert Lea as a downburst likely caused a home to be destroyed. The flash flooding in Mankato and other areas of Blue Earth County damaged an estimated 215 homes, mostly due to water seepage into basements, but about 15-20 homes had cracked or otherwise damaged foundations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 24 hour measured rainfall amount of 3.51 inches in North Mankato.   All of the rain fell late on the 17th and during the early morning hours of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms began during the week of June 14th, and continued through most of the week with areas of flash flooding. The flash flooding evolved into areal flooding, and then main-stem river flooding which continued through the end of the month. The hardest hit areas were from New Ulm to Mankato, northeast through the length of the Minnesota River, and the Twin Cities Metro area which had major problems associated with Lake Minnetonka that drains eastward across the cities (Minnehaha Creek), and into the Mississippi near St. Paul. Plus, area lakes continued to rise which created more problems with lake houses inundated with the rising lake levels.||Parts of Minnesota received 10-12 inches of rain from June 14th through the 23rd, which is 400-600% above normal. In addition, the Twin Cities had their wettest start of the year since 1871. Local damage assessments initially reported flood damaged homes, businesses, roads, and infrastructure across a large part southern Minnesota. ||The Minnesota State Emergency Operations Center was partially activated on June 16, and then was fully activated on June 18.  On June 19, the Governor of Minnesota declared a State of Emergency for 35 counties. ||During the height of the flooding the following were impacts according from local officials:||Scott County: Multiple reports of road closures due to high water and debris.  Sporadic power outages reported throughout the county.  Mudslides in Blakeley closed the train tracks in the area, impacting Union Pacific and Canadian Pacific rail operations. Highways 6, 41 and 101 were closed due to the Minnesota River flooding. ||Carver County:  More than 30 local and county roads were closed due to flooding, including around the communities of Chaska, Mayer, Hollywood, Camden, Dahlgren, Young America and Watertown. During the height of the flooding, all river crossings along the Minnesota River were closed. ||Faribault County: Multiple local and county roads were closed; there were some impacts to wastewater treatment plants and drinking water systems.||Rice County: A Faribault wastewater sludge pond overtopped.||St. Paul Airport deployed a flood wall, which closed two of their shorter runways. ||Minneapolis: A 100-yard mudslide occurred at the edge of the Fairview-University of Minnesota Hospital in Minneapolis.  No structural damage occurred and engineers determined that there was no threat to the hospital.  Debris from the mudslide flowed into the Mississippi River and caused problems downstream at the locks and dams.||Sibley County: Mudslides were going into some residences. The City of Henderson was sandbagging due to flood waters.||Dakota County: The City of Mendota was evacuated due to a large mudslide that could have potentially affected several homes.  MnDOT closed one lane on 35W due to the Minnesota River flood waters.  Due to the flood waters affecting downtown St. Paul and Harriet Island, the Taste of Minnesota and the 4th of July Fireworks were moved to a different location. ||Wright County:  Water levels rose in Delano. The city constructed a temporary clay levee to mitigate flooding issues.  The primary impacts were flooding of low-level roads and minor basement flooding that began to affect homes closest to the river.  Water levels of the Crow River at Delano were the highest in 50 years.||Mankato: Flooding caused the evacuation of an apartment complex.||Brown County; Highway 13 that crosses the Minnesota River was closed due to flood waters.||The Minnesota State Patrol reported that Highway 169 between Mankato and St. Peter, from milepost 61 to 64, was closed due to a debris flow of mud and trees.||In Edina/St. Louis Park; Flood waters approached the Methodist Hospital due to the swollen Minnehaha Creek. Several streets along Minnehaha Creek in St. Louis Park were closed due to flood waters.||The following were some of the roads closed in south central Minnesota:|Hwy 19 west of Gaylord (Sibley County)|Hwy 19 east of Henderson (Sibley County)|Hwy 93 south of Henderson (Sibley County)|Hwy 4 between Sleepy Eye and Fairfax (Brown, Nicollet, Renville Counties)|Hwy 22 north of Gaylord (Sibley County)|Hwy 112 west of LeCenter (Le Sueur County)|Hwy 169 SB between St. Peter and LeSueur (Le Sueur County)|Hwy 13 near Kilkenny (Le Sueur County)|Hwy 14 west of Springfield (Brown County)|Hwy 19 west of Henderson (Sibley County)|Hwy 83 north of St. Clair (Blue Earth County).||Flood damage to public structures during Minnesota���s rainiest June on record stands at $48 million.  The following are some individual counties and cost assessment: ||Blue Earth, $2.3M||Brown, $850,000||Carver, $9.2M||Chippewa, $75,000||Faribault, $150,000||Freeborn, $625,000||Hennepin, $4.0M||Le Sueur, $1.1M||McLeod, $300,000||Nicollet, $500,000||Ramsey, $1.9M||Renville, $750,000||Scott, $9.0M||Sibley, $3,9M||Steele, $2.4M||Waseca, $30,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources in Carver County compiled a summary of flood related damages and locations for the flood of June 2014. More than 30 local and county roads were closed due to flooding, including the communities of Chaska, Mayer, Hollywood, Camden, Dahlgren, Young America, Laketown and Watertown. ||The Spring Creek levee failed in Carver. It caused significant mud slides affecting five private properties. In Cologne, 3 private residents had basement flooding. In Victoria, 6-8 homes had flooded basements. In Waconia, 4 homes had flooded basements. ||There were numerous county parks closed and several area lakes had no wake zones due to high water levels. ||During the height of the flooding, all river crossings along the Minnesota River were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of July 6th. These storms formed along a warm front that was lifting northeast through the region. Some of these storms contained strong winds that blew down trees and  power lines from Homer to Pickwick (Winona County). A house in Homer sustained damage when a tree landed on it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down between Pickwick and Homer. One home was damaged when a tree fell on it and one lane of U.S. Highway 61 was closed for a short time due to debris that was blown onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northeast Minnesota, on Monday (July 21st).  A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin.  Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s.  Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex.  This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and the northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd).  This derecho caused widespread wind damage from winds of at least 50 to 70 mph over much of northeast Minnesota, blowing and/or snapping trees and knocking out power.  There were more focused areas of structural damage that suggested there were winds in excess of 70 mph.  There were several injuries to campers in the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness due to falling trees, which required officials to make some rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.  One tree was 12 inches in diameter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources in Scott County compiled a summary of flood related damages and locations for the flood of June 2014. There were multiple reports of road closures due to high water and debris. Sporadic power outages reported throughout the county due to the water and electrical equipment being underwater. Mudslides and flood waters cut off all access to the town of Blakeley. This included train tracks that impacted Union Pacific and Canadian Pacific rail operations. Highways 6, 41 and 101 were all closed from flood waters of the Minnesota River. The town of Louisville, north of Jordan and west of Prior Lake, had two homes surrounded by flood waters during the height of the flood. Highway 169 was the only access from Scott County during the height of the flooding. In addition, no wake zones were posted on area lakes due to high levels and several parks were closed. Due to high levels on Prior Lake, County Road 21 was closed near the downtown area. In addition, numerous homes along the lake started to sandbag due to rising lake levels. In Shakopee, three rides were shut down at Valleyfair because of floodwaters that spilled into the park and across its parking lots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northeast Minnesota, on Monday (July 21st).  A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin.  Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s.  Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex.  This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and the northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd).  This derecho caused widespread wind damage from winds of at least 50 to 70 mph over much of northeast Minnesota, blowing and/or snapping trees and knocking out power.  There were more focused areas of structural damage that suggested there were winds in excess of 70 mph.  There were several injuries to campers in the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness due to falling trees, which required officials to make some rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees between 6 and 12 inches in diameter were blown down in Hoyt Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early morning hours of the 4th, an area of surface low pressure had moved into northeast South Dakota, with a warm front extending out to the east. Surface dew point values north of the front ranged in the lower 60s while south of the front they topped out around 70. At 850 millibars, the low level jet pushed juicy air up into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, with drier air to the north. Finally, forty-five to fifty knot westerly winds at 500 millibars provided ample shear to the system. Thunderstorms over western and central North Dakota proceeded east and moved into Barnes County, North Dakota, during the first few hours of the 4th. These storms tracked nearly due east, finally exiting Wadena County, Minnesota, around sunrise. The strongest storms mainly stayed along and south of Interstate 94 in North Dakota and U. S. Highway 10 in Minnesota. These storms trained across the same areas as well, with some spots experiencing large hail or damaging winds two or three times during the event. In addition, heavy rainfall became a problem, resulting in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A truck was blown off highway 210 near Battle Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 5 to 10 inch poplar trees were broken and laying on the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources in Hennepin County compiled a summary of flood related damages and locations for the flood of June 2014. In Minneapolis a 100-yard mudslide occurred at the edge of the Fairview-University of Minnesota Hospital. No structural damage occurred and engineers determined that there was no threat to the hospital. Debris from this mudslide did flow into the Mississippi River and caused problems downstream at the locks and dams. In Edina and St. Louis Park, flood waters approached Methodist Hospital due to the swollen Minnehaha Creek. Several streets along Minnehaha Creek were flooded from the headwaters near Woodland, downstream to the Mississippi River. Numerous homes and businesses along the Minnehaha Creek were sandbagged due to the potential of being flooded. In addition, area lakes had a no wake zone for boats due to very high lake levels. Local officials indicated that in the city of Edina, 48 homes had flood damage, both from creek and other flooding, as well as from sewer backups.||The record rainfall on Thursday, June 19 caused Lake Minnetonka���s water level to reach an all-time high of 930.77 feet above sea level, breaking the previous record of 930.66 feet set on June 3, 2014.||In addition to the high lake levels in Hennepin County, 40 homes in the city of Mound were uninhabitable due to flood-related sewage backups near Lake Minnetonka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of weak tornadoes and funnel clouds developed along a line of thunderstorms across west central Minnesota during the late afternoon of Thursday, June 19th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Saturday, September 20th, a complex of thunderstorms developed across eastern North Dakota, and moved southeast across west central Minnesota by the afternoon, and through most of southern Minnesota by the early evening. Embedded thunderstorms formed lines and caused severe wind gusts from west central, southeast to southeast Minnesota. Most of the gusts were between 40 and 55 mph, but a few gusted over 60 mph and caused wind damage. In addition, hail formed near Waseca, and dropped a considerable amount in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 inch wide apple tree was uprooted. There were also several other reporting sites that had wind gusts over 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northeast Minnesota, on Monday (July 21st).  A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin.  Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s.  Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex.  This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and the northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd).  This derecho caused widespread wind damage from winds of at least 50 to 70 mph over much of northeast Minnesota, blowing and/or snapping trees and knocking out power.  There were more focused areas of structural damage that suggested there were winds in excess of 70 mph.  There were several injuries to campers in the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness due to falling trees, which required officials to make some rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down throughout the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of road closures due to high water and debris along the Minnesota River. In the southeast part of Le Sueur County, some residents in the town of Waterville, Minnesota had to evacuate their homes along Lillian Street due to rising waters of the local lake. The city council declared the town a disaster area and had volunteers help them fill sandbags to stop further damage to homes. As lake levels rose, several homes were surrounded by water from the nearby lakes, and a few will be completely destroyed. The city closed four streets and parts of three others due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Saturday, September 20th, a complex of thunderstorms developed across eastern North Dakota, and moved southeast across west central Minnesota by the afternoon, and through most of southern Minnesota by the early evening. Embedded thunderstorms formed lines and caused severe wind gusts from west central, southeast to southeast Minnesota. Most of the gusts were between 40 and 55 mph, but a few gusted over 60 mph and caused wind damage. In addition, hail formed near Waseca, and dropped a considerable amount in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down across Highway 21 north of New Ulm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds brought hot and muggy weather conditions to the northern Plains and Upper Midwest, including northeast Minnesota, on Monday (July 21st).  A low pressure system was located in north central South Dakota by Monday evening, and its stationary front extended to the northeast through eastern North Dakota and Northwest Wisconsin.  Temperatures were in the middle 80s to low 90s in the warm sector, and the combination of dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to middle 70s resulted in heat indices in the 90s to 100s.  Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening in western North Dakota and formed a mesoscale convective complex.  This MCV then became a derecho Monday evening as it moved and bowed east through eastern North Dakota and the northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin late Monday and early Tuesday (July 22nd).  This derecho caused widespread wind damage from winds of at least 50 to 70 mph over much of northeast Minnesota, blowing and/or snapping trees and knocking out power.  There were more focused areas of structural damage that suggested there were winds in excess of 70 mph.  There were several injuries to campers in the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness due to falling trees, which required officials to make some rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought large hail to parts of northeast Minnesota on June 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell for 2 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was piled up in town, with some areas up to 6 inches deep in the town of Waseca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Monday, June 16th, two major thunderstorm complexes across Nebraska and South Dakota, congealed into one along the Minnesota, Iowa border from Sioux Falls, eastward along I-90 to Fairmont and Albert Lea. Some of the severe storms moved north of I-90 and produced wind and hail as far north as Mankato, Waseca and Owatonna.  Wind gusts in excess of 60 to 70 mph were clocked in these areas and produced numerous large trees down, power outages, and significant street flooding and erosion due to two to four inches of rainfall in less than two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey concluded that severe straight-line winds caused damage to half of a metal building system north of Minnesota Lake. The building's metal wall and roof panels were peeled off with most of the northern half of the building collapsed. Empty grain bins nearby were bent or tipped. Two homes nearby sustained minor wind damage to shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weeks of heavy rains over and near Lake Kabetogama caused the water level to relatively quickly rise about 2 feet during the month of June.  The flooding inundated docks and threatened, damaged, and destroyed lakeshore property, especially due to wave action from strong north and east winds.  For perspective, the water level was at about 17.0 feet during the middle of May and steadily increased to about 18.5 feet by early June due to the heavy rains in late May. It then quickly rose about 2 feet over the following couple weeks due to additional heavy rains.  The water level crested at about 20.65 early on June 19th before slowly receding through the rest of the month.  The water level receded to about 19.73 feet by the end of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water levels on Lake Kabetogama inundated docks and threatened, damaged, and destroyed lakeshore property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of weak tornadoes and funnel clouds developed along a line of thunderstorms across west central Minnesota during the late afternoon of Thursday, June 19th. All tornadoes and funnel clouds were quite narrow, and some of them occurred simultaneously or nearly so.  There was no significant damage nor any injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long and skinny funnel cloud developed north of Danvers. It appeared to come close to the ground but nobody saw it touch down nor was there any known damage. Just as the funnel was dissipating, a tornado did develop at 1550 CST north of Danvers very close to where the funnel had been.

Thunderstorms developed late Tuesday evening along an elevated frontal boundary across far southern Minnesota. Initially, torrential rainfall caused flooding around New Ulm and Mankato, then it evolved into a wind damage threat as it moved eastward across south central Minnesota. Significant damage occurred in Albert Lea as a downburst likely caused a home to be destroyed. The flash flooding in Mankato and other areas of Blue Earth County damaged an estimated 215 homes, mostly due to water seepage into basements, but about 15-20 homes had cracked or otherwise damaged foundations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early morning hours of the 4th, an area of surface low pressure had moved into northeast South Dakota, with a warm front extending out to the east. Surface dew point values north of the front ranged in the lower 60s while south of the front they topped out around 70. At 850 millibars, the low level jet pushed juicy air up into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, with drier air to the north. Finally, forty-five to fifty knot westerly winds at 500 millibars provided ample shear to the system. Thunderstorms over western and central North Dakota proceeded east and moved into Barnes County, North Dakota, during the first few hours of the 4th. These storms tracked nearly due east, finally exiting Wadena County, Minnesota, around sunrise. The strongest storms mainly stayed along and south of Interstate 94 in North Dakota and U. S. Highway 10 in Minnesota. These storms trained across the same areas as well, with some spots experiencing large hail or damaging winds two or three times during the event. In addition, heavy rainfall became a problem, resulting in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell at Big McDonald Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided in the first week of the month along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late June on the Mississippi River in Wabasha ended in the first week of July when the river went below the flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided in the first week of the month along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late June on the Mississippi River in Winona ended in the first week of July when the river went below the flood stage. The river crested 2.5 feet above the flood stage at 15.52 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided in the first week of the month along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Mississippi River, combined with runoff from heavy rain, caused flooding to occur on the west side of Wabasha. This flooding prompted the Minnesota Department of Transportation to close State Highway 60 in late June. The road was opened in the first week July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers that went over flood stage in late June from excessive runoff, remained in flood to begin July. The flooding subsided in the first week of the month along the Mississippi River.
