Accumulations, generally over a 14 to 18 hour period, varied from 3 to 8 inches. The snow was accompanied by relatively light winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches, including 3.5 inches 4 miles north of Currie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow spread over southwest Minnesota fom the west from the predawn hours through the morning of November 15th, and continued through the afternoon and into the evening of the same day. Accumulations, generally over a 14 to 18 hour period, varied from 3 to 8 inches. The snow was accompanied by relatively light winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms occurred Thursday afternoon and evening as warm air surged northward across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest. A warm front extended across far southern Minnesota.  Two tornadoes formed along this front near St. James and Butternut, and moved northeast across parts of Watonwan and Blue Earth Counties. Another complex of severe thunderstorms developed near Waseca, and moved northeast across the southern Metro area, and into west central Wisconsin. Several reports of damaging winds occurred with this bow type thunderstorm complex Thursday afternoon.

The afternoon of Monday, June 16th, two major thunderstorm complexes across Nebraska and South Dakota, congealed into one along the Minnesota, Iowa border from Sioux Falls, eastward along I-90 to Fairmont and Albert Lea. Some of the severe storms moved north of I-90 and produced wind and hail as far north as Mankato, Waseca and Owatonna.  Wind gusts in excess of 60 to 70 mph were clocked in these areas and produced numerous large trees down, power outages, and significant street flooding and erosion due to two to four inches of rainfall in less than two hours.
Thunderstorms formed along and ahead of a cold front that was located over central North Dakota late in the afternoon of the 21st. The thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straightline wind damage occurred in town with shingles blown off homes, trees uprooted, and hail damage to siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed early Wednesday morning, September 3rd in southeast South Dakota, and moved northeast into southwest Minnesota. Some of the thunderstorms became severe as they moved eastward across Renville County, and into the western portions of the Twin Cities. Two inch elongated hail was reported near Hector, and hail damaged an apple orchard in Wright County.
There were thunderstorms over northeast Minnesota on May 7th.  Some storms produced large hail.

Thunderstorms produced numerous events of large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and areal flooding across most of southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 16th. The areal flooding was a longer term event and persisted through much of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of fields and other lowlands, including several roads, homes, and businesses. This flooding lasted for two days, and was aggravated locally by additional storms during that time. Some roads were damaged or washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along and ahead of a cold front that was located over central North Dakota late in the afternoon of the 21st. The thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 8 inch diameter trees were snapped near the ground in a cemetary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed late Tuesday evening along an elevated frontal boundary across far southern Minnesota. Initially, torrential rainfall caused flooding around New Ulm and Mankato, then it evolved into a wind damage threat as it moved eastward across south central Minnesota. Significant damage occurred in Albert Lea as a downburst likely caused a home to be destroyed. The flash flooding in Mankato and other areas of Blue Earth County damaged an estimated 215 homes, mostly due to water seepage into basements, but about 15-20 homes had cracked or otherwise damaged foundations.
A line of thunderstorms moved into central Roseau County late in the afternoon of the 24th. One cell became severe and produced quarter sized hail and very heavy rain just south of Roseau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of pea sized hail with some quarter sized hail mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over northeast Minnesota dropped damaging hail to some areas of northeast Minnesota on July 26th.  In some cases, the hail was quite large.  One storm dropped hail 2 inches in diameter, nearly the size of billiard balls, to a part of rural Aitkin County, which caused minor structural and agricultural damage.
An isolated thunderstorm brought quarter-size hail to part of Big Stone county.
Thunderstorms produced heavy rain, flash flooding, and longer term areal flooding over the southwestern corner of Minnesota from the late evening of June 14th into the daytime hours of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of some fields and other lowlands, including several roads. This flooding lasted well into the daytime hours after the rain subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota on April 12th. These storms developed along a very slow moving cold front and primarily produced large hail, although some occasional high winds also were reported. The first round of storms moved in during the mid morning hours with the last of the storms ending during the early evening. Most of the hail that fell during the day was quarter sized or smaller.

A few thunderstorms developed near the Iowa/Minnesota border during the early evening of Wednesday, May 7th. Some of the storms quickly became severe and produced severe hail near Madelia. Other strong storms produced non-severe hail across central Minnesota where there were several reports of 3/4 inch hail. By the late evening, a cluster of strong thunderstorms near Mankato quickly became severe and moved slowly east and northeast across portions of south central and east central Minnesota, before weakening near the Twin Cities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of dime size hail from Eagan to Inver Grove Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds in Pipestone and Murray Counties in southwest Minnesota shortly after midnight on May 8th.

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 7th. These storms became severe as they moved from central to east central Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. A large hail swath occurred in northern Scott County and moved eastward into portions of northwest and central Dakota County. In addition, there was a brief tornado touchdown in southern Washington County at the St. Croix Bluffs Regional Park. The damage path was small and mainly associated with downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six inch diameter tree was blown down and blocked a road in Burnsville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 45 was blocked due to water from a local creek spilling over the roadway south of Owatonna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early evening of Friday, June 20th, a severe thunderstorm moved across west central Minnesota, into portions of central Minnesota near Wadena, and moved east-southeast across northern Todd County, producing hail and a funnel cloud.

Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front over southeast Minnesota during the evening of August 17th. These storms produced heavy rain that caused street flooding to occur in Rochester (Olmsted County) with the water reaching the bumpers on some vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred on the northwest side of Rochester with the water reaching the bumpers on some vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front during the late afternoon hours of August 18th across southeast Minnesota. These storms blew down some power lines and trees in the city of Winona (Winona County) and dropped golf ball sized hail north of Genoa (Olmsted County) that damaged vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and some trees were blown down hear Huff Street and U.S. Highway 61 in the city of Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of an advancing cold front during the afternoon of August 29th. Some of these storms across southeast Minnesota contained high winds that blew down trees at a campground near Elba (Winona County), damaged trees in the city of Winona (Winona County) and bent a power pole in Rushford (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at a campground near Elba. The high winds also damaged a picnic shelter, destroyed a storage shed and one of the fallen trees went through the roof of a trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rolled across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of August 4th. A lightning strike from one of these storms hit a transformer in La Crescent (Houston County). The damage from the strike was enough to make a nearby substation unrepairable causing around 3,400 customers to be without power until the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit a transformer in La Crescent and the resulting damage also took out a nearby substation. Around 3,400 customers were without power until the next day.

These storms blew down some power lines and trees in the city of Winona (Winona County) and dropped golf ball sized hail north of Genoa (Olmsted County) that damaged vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell north of Genoa that damaged vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust occurred in Eyota.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was bent by wind gusts along State Highway 43 in Rushford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced the visibility to a half mile or less during the early morning hours of August 5th across southeast Minnesota. A car rolled off a Rochester (Olmsted County) road in the fog, injuring one of the occupants of the vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was injured when a car rolled off a Rochester road. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport indicated dense fog with a visibility of a half mile or less was occurring at the time of the accident.

Some of these storms across southeast Minnesota contained high winds that blew down trees at a campground near Elba (Winona County), damaged trees in the city of Winona (Winona County) and bent a power pole in Rushford (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph occurred on the west side of Winona that blew down some tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of August 25th across southeast Minnesota along a nearly stationary front. These storms dropped quarter sized hail in Le Roy (Mower County) and nickel sized hail in Preston (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Le Roy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of August 25th across southeast Minnesota along a nearly stationary front. These storms dropped quarter sized hail in Le Roy (Mower County) and nickel sized hail in Preston (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail fell in Preston. The power was also knocked out, but the cause was not known.
